narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a white male applicant with a solid undergraduate GPA of 3.70 and a middle-class family background, you've clearly put in significant effort to build a strong profile for law school. Your Year 1 and Year 3 school deciles both sit at 6th, which is right around or slightly above average for students in our dataset, and you're planning to attend full-time, just like the vast majority of applicants. However, the admissions model, which predicts the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated your probability of failure at 65.9%—a threshold that unfortunately led to the rejection of your application, as the school prioritizes candidates predicted to succeed. The most influential factor pushing toward this failure prediction was your LSAT score of 34, which is below the typical 36.7 average and stood out as the strongest signal of risk to the model. Next in importance, your Year 1 decile of 6 nudged the prediction further toward failure compared to what the model expects from top performers. Your middle family income bracket also contributed somewhat to this direction, as it aligns with patterns the model associates with higher bar exam challenges. On a more positive note, your Year 3 decile provided a counterbalance, slightly favoring a pass prediction since it matches average levels well. Your strong undergraduate GPA and full-time status offered minor boosts toward success, but they weren't enough to outweigh the top concerns. Compared to typical students who get approved, your LSAT is noticeably lower, while your other metrics like GPA and deciles are competitive or average, highlighting where the model saw the biggest gap. This isn't a judgment on your potential—it's just one data-driven view based on historical patterns. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost that score, strengthening your overall academic narrative, and reapplying to schools with more flexible thresholds or bolstering your application with additional experiences like internships. We're rooting for your success, and with targeted improvements, you can turn this around. Keep pushing ahead.
